edges.description: Loss of the ABCC6 transporter abolishes its normal promotion of cellular ATP efflux.
Reduced extracellular ATP lowers the substrate available for conversion to inorganic pyrophosphate.
Loss of the PPi anti-mineralization brake permits calcium-phosphate deposition on elastic fibers.
Mineralization of arterial elastic laminae drives the systemic vasculopathy.
Dystrophic calcification of Bruch membrane produces angioid streaks.
Angioid streaks in calcified Bruch membrane can trigger choroidal neovascularization.
intermediate_mechanisms: Angioid streaks in calcified Bruch membrane

edges.description: Ocular mineralization complications cause reduced central visual acuity.
Mineralized mid-dermal elastic fibers produce yellow papules in flexural skin.
Progressive dermal elastic-fiber mineralization causes loose and wrinkled skin.
Small- and medium-artery lesions narrow peripheral arterial supply and cause claudication.
edges.target: Intermittent Claudication and Peripheral Arterial Disease

edges.description: PXE vasculopathy is defined by calcification of arterial elastic fibers.
Coronary involvement in systemic PXE vasculopathy can cause angina or myocardial infarction.
Vascular fragility in the digestive tract predisposes to GI bleeding.
Cerebrovascular disease in systemic PXE vasculopathy can cause stroke.
Renal arterial involvement can produce renovascular hypertension.

nodes.description: PXE is caused by biallelic loss-of-function variants in ABCC6, an ATP-binding cassette transmembrane transporter expressed mainly in hepatocytes. Loss of functional ABCC6 protein is the primary molecular defect of the disease.

evidence.reference_title: Pseudoxanthoma elasticum - Genetics, pathophysiology, and clinical presentation.
evidence.snippet: Mutations in the ABCC6-gene are causative, coding for a transmembrane transporter mainly expressed in hepatocytes, which promotes the efflux of adenosine triphosphate (ATP).

nodes.description: Breaks in the calcified, brittle Bruch membrane produce angioid streaks, a characteristic ocular sign that can trigger choroidal neovascularization.

evidence.snippet: Dystrophic calcification of Bruch's membrane, revealed by angioid streaks, may trigger choroidal neovascularization

nodes.description: Intravitreal vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) inhibitor therapy is used to treat macular/choroidal neovascularization, the main threat to vision in PXE.

evidence.snippet: the main symptomatic treatments are vascular endothelial growth factor inhibitor therapy (for ophthalmic manifestations), lifestyle, lipid-lowering and dietary measures (for reducing vascular risk factors), and vascular surgery

nodes.description: Calcification of elastic fibers within arterial walls is a defining cardiovascular feature of PXE.

evidence.reference_title: Pseudoxanthoma elasticum - Genetics, pathophysiology, and clinical presentation.
evidence.snippet: The clinical phenotype of PXE is characterized by the effects of elastic fiber calcification in the skin, the cardiovascular system, and the eyes.

nodes.description: Calcification of elastic fibers in small and medium-sized arteries produces a systemic vasculopathy. Luminal compromise and vessel fragility underlie peripheral arterial disease, gastrointestinal bleeding, ischemic stroke, renovascular hypertension, and cardiac ischemia.

evidence.reference_title: Pseudoxanthoma elasticum - Genetics, pathophysiology, and clinical presentation.
evidence.snippet: The clinical phenotype of PXE is characterized by the effects of elastic fiber calcification in the skin, the cardiovascular system, and the eyes.
Lesions in small and medium-sized artery walls may result in intermittent claudication and peripheral artery disease.

nodes.description: Coronary involvement can produce angina pectoris and myocardial infarction; cardiac complications are considered relatively uncommon but clinically important.

evidence.snippet: Cardiac complications (myocardial infarction, angina pectoris) are thought to be relatively rare but merit thorough investigation.

nodes.description: Secondary choroidal neovascularization with macular complications is the leading cause of central vision loss in PXE.

evidence.snippet: Dystrophic calcification of Bruch's membrane, revealed by angioid streaks, may trigger choroidal neovascularization

nodes.description: Small yellow papules on the nape and lateral neck and in flexural areas are typically the first clinical sign; they coalesce, and the skin becomes loose and wrinkled.

evidence.snippet: The first clinical sign of PXE is almost always small yellow papules on the nape and sides of the neck and in flexural areas.

nodes.description: Without sufficient PPi, calcium and phosphate progressively deposit on elastic fibers in the mid-dermis, Bruch membrane of the eye, and arterial walls. The affected elastic fibers become fragmented, clumped, and calcified (demonstrable on skin biopsy with a von Kossa calcium stain).

evidence.snippet: The lack of functional ABCC6 protein leads to ectopic mineralization that is most apparent in the elastic tissues of the skin, eyes and blood vessels.
The mid-dermal elastic fibers are short, fragmented, clumped and calcified.

nodes.description: Vascular fragility predisposes to premature gastrointestinal angina and bleeding, particularly from the upper gastrointestinal tract.

evidence.snippet: Other manifestations include premature gastrointestinal angina and/or bleeding, intermittent claudication of arm and leg muscles, stroke, renovascular hypertension, and cardiovascular complications (angina/myocardial infarction).

nodes.description: Calcific lesions of small and medium-sized arteries cause peripheral arterial disease with intermittent claudication of the arm and leg muscles.
nodes.id: Intermittent Claudication and Peripheral Arterial Disease

evidence.snippet: Lesions in small and medium-sized artery walls may result in intermittent claudication and peripheral artery disease.

nodes.description: Cerebrovascular involvement can cause ischemic stroke as part of the systemic vasculopathy.

evidence.snippet: Other manifestations include premature gastrointestinal angina and/or bleeding, intermittent claudication of arm and leg muscles, stroke, renovascular hypertension, and cardiovascular complications (angina/myocardial infarction).

nodes.description: Extracellular ATP is normally converted to inorganic pyrophosphate (PPi), the principal circulating anti-mineralization factor. Reduced hepatic ATP release lowers plasma PPi and the PPi/Pi ratio, removing the brake on tissue calcification.

evidence.reference_title: Pseudoxanthoma elasticum - Genetics, pathophysiology, and clinical presentation.
evidence.snippet: This results in low levels of plasma inorganic pyrophosphate (PPi), a critical anti-mineralization factor.

There is good evidence to suggest that the factor is inorganic pyrophosphate (PPi), and that the circulating low levels of PPi and decreased PPi/Pi ratio result from the lack of ATP release by hepatocytes harboring the mutant ABCC6 protein.

nodes.description: Functional ABCC6 normally promotes cellular efflux of ATP from hepatocytes into the circulation. With ABCC6 loss, hepatic ATP release falls, reducing the extracellular ATP substrate available for downstream conversion.

evidence.reference_title: ABCC6, Pyrophosphate and Ectopic Calcification: Therapeutic Solutions
evidence.snippet: ABCC6 facilitates the cellular efflux of ATP, which is rapidly converted into inorganic pyrophosphate (PPi) and adenosine by the ectonucleotidases NPP1 and CD73 (NT5E).

nodes.description: Subretinal neovascularization and macular atrophy reduce visual acuity and are the most frequent cause of morbidity and disability in PXE.

evidence.snippet: The most frequent cause of morbidity and disability in PXE is reduced vision due to complications of subretinal neovascularizations and macular atrophy.

nodes.description: Calcific renal arterial disease can produce renovascular hypertension.

evidence.snippet: Other manifestations include premature gastrointestinal angina and/or bleeding, intermittent claudication of arm and leg muscles, stroke, renovascular hypertension, and cardiovascular complications (angina/myocardial infarction).

nodes.description: With disease progression the coalescing papules give way to lax, loose, and wrinkled skin in affected flexural areas, a characteristic feature of established PXE.

evidence.snippet: The papules coalesce, and the skin becomes loose and wrinkled.
